q: A venue manager's card is used by three different staff members across a week. What can finance prove about a $180 charge that shows up?
options: Exactly who spent it and why
Nothing — there's no way to attribute it to a person
Only the general ledger code
Only the time of day
explanation: Shared cards eliminate attribution entirely — the information needed to trace spend to a person was never captured in the first place.

q: Why does month-end reconciliation blow out in most hospitality groups?
options: Because staff are dishonest
Because a high volume of small, receipt-less purchases across every venue land on finance all at once
Because the accounting software is outdated
Because head office is too small
explanation: It's a volume problem, not a character problem — dozens of small, urgent purchases across every venue all need reconciling at once.

q: Why is per-venue GL coding routinely wrong in multi-site hospitality groups?
options: The software doesn't support cost centres
Venue staff aren't finance people and are coding on the fly, not head office
Head office refuses to set up cost centres
It's only wrong at newly opened venues
explanation: Coding gets pushed down to people whose job isn't finance, under time pressure — errors are the predictable result, not the exception.

q: A venue manager resigns with no notice. What's the biggest risk to spend control in the days that follow?
options: The venue will close early
Their card, float and any unresolved receipts get passed around informally or simply disappear
Head office will automatically reissue a card within the hour
There is no risk — payroll handles it
explanation: Without instant card cancellation and reissue, a manager's departure leaves a gap that gets filled informally — exactly where control breaks down.

q: Why doesn't a "budget policy" reviewed monthly actually stop bad spend?
options: Because staff don't read policies
Because the money has already left the business by the time anyone reviews it
Because policies are only for reimbursements
Because it's reviewed too often
explanation: A control applied after the money's gone can only document spend — it can't prevent it. Real controls sit at the point of sale.

q: What's the most common physical-world equivalent of a shared card in a hospitality venue?
options: A company laptop
A petty cash tin passed between shifts
A supplier invoice
A rostering app
explanation: Petty cash tins have the exact same attribution problem as shared cards, just in physical form.

q: The classic small, urgent hospitality purchase — a mid-service dash to the supermarket for a missing ingredient — is sometimes nicknamed the "___________ run."
hint: A common salad ingredient used as shorthand for this kind of purchase.
explanation: "The lettuce run" is shorthand some hospitality groups use for the mid-shift dash to buy a missing ingredient — a perfect example of small, urgent, receipt-less spend.

q: Hospitality has some of the highest staff ___________ of any industry, and venue managers are exactly the people holding the cards.
hint: The rate at which staff leave and get replaced.
explanation: High staff turnover means card handoffs happen constantly — and every handoff is a fresh chance for spend to go unattributed.

q: Budget and category rules that are only checked in a monthly review are a ___________ control, not a real one.
explanation: A post-hoc control can only document spend that already happened — it can't stop it.

context: Ops manager at a ~200-location QSR chain: “Head office handles all of that from our support office.”
options: “Got it — so your finance team is at head office?”
“Got it — so when a restaurant needs something urgent, how does the support office find out?”
“That sounds like a centralized model.”
“Do you mean your corporate office?”
explanation: Mirroring their own term ("support office," not "head office" or "corporate") signals you're already fluent in how they talk about their business — a small thing that builds instant credibility.

context: Franchise group finance lead: “Each of our stores has its own store card, but a couple have gone missing and staff don't always have a phone on them to freeze it.”
options: “You should really upgrade everyone's phones.”
“That's exactly the gap physical cards frozen centrally solves — you don't need a phone at the store, head office can freeze it the moment it's reported.”
“Most groups just accept a few lost cards a year.”
“Have you thought about credit cards instead?”
explanation: This mirrors a real objection pattern (lost store cards, no phone per store) with the exact fix — central freezing, no dependency on store-level tech.

context: CFO: “We already declined AP automation when a vendor pitched it earlier this year — not interested in another tool.”
options: “That's fine, let's talk about AP anyway.”
“Totally understand — AP is usually the last thing hospitality groups want first. Can I ask about cards and reimbursements instead? That's usually where the real pain is.”
“AP is actually our best feature, let me show you.”
“You should reconsider that decision.”
explanation: Nobody wants AP first in this vertical — land cards and reimbursements, and revisit AP after rollout rather than fighting the objection.
